findings.description: The Overview states 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput' but Stage 2 explicitly states 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.' This directly contradicts the document's description of its fundamental execution model.
findings.evidence: Overview: "All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput." Stage 2: "Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins."
findings.suggestion: Correct the Overview to accurately describe the pipeline execution model — stages are not all parallel; Stage 2→3 is sequential.

findings.description: The document references 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6' but no Section 6 exists. The document has no security section whatsoever — a significant structural gap for a final-stage architecture brief on a production ETL system.
findings.evidence: "Security considerations are documented in Section 6." Document ends at Conclusions. No Section 6 exists.
findings.suggestion: Add a Security section covering: authentication method, authorization model, encryption in transit and at rest. Remove or update the dangling reference.

findings.description: The claim that the in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% is implausible. If the cached system achieves sub-second latency (per Performance section), then the uncached system would have ~1000-second latency — inconsistent with any functioning production pipeline.
findings.evidence: "The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes." + "the pipeline maintains sub-second end-to-end latency for 95th-percentile events."
findings.suggestion: Replace with a measured, defensible figure and cite the baseline. Typical caching improvements for this workload type are 50–95%.

findings.description: The claim of sub-second end-to-end latency is directly contradicted by the 5-minute polling interval for batch completion in Stage 3. A 5-minute polling interval means batch completion latency is up to 5 minutes, not sub-second.
findings.evidence: "Polling for batch completion uses a 5-minute interval." vs "the pipeline maintains sub-second end-to-end latency for 95th-percentile events."
findings.suggestion: Reconcile: either reduce polling interval to seconds, or scope the latency claim to exclude batch completion acknowledgment latency.

findings.description: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' No retry policy is defined in this document or referenced. Engineers implementing or operating this stage cannot use this description.
findings.evidence: "Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures." No definition of 'standard retry policy' exists anywhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Define the retry policy inline (max attempts, backoff strategy, timeout) or reference the document that specifies it.

findings.description: Benchmark claim that 'batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB' is presented without methodology, environment, or baseline. The comparison direction is architecturally unusual and may be inverted.
findings.evidence: "Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB. For larger payloads, streaming is preferred." No test conditions cited.
findings.suggestion: Verify and cite the benchmark methodology. Clarify whether the size threshold and comparison direction are correct.

findings.description: The overflow queue for large files is introduced but its downstream handling is never described. Stage 3 delivery only describes the primary path — large files on the overflow queue have no documented destination.
findings.evidence: "Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue." No further mention of overflow queue in Stage 3 or elsewhere.
findings.suggestion: Describe the overflow queue's downstream handling — does it feed into Stage 3 via a different path? Is there a separate delivery mechanism?

findings.description: Performance section describes streaming in present tense as current behavior ('For larger payloads, streaming is preferred'), while Conclusions describes real-time streaming as 'Future work.' The document contradicts itself on whether streaming is supported today.
findings.evidence: "For larger payloads, streaming is preferred." vs "Future work includes adding a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline."
findings.suggestion: Clarify: if streaming is already supported, remove it from Future work. If it is planned, use future tense in the Performance section.

findings.description: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' No mechanism is named. An architecture brief should specify the authentication method.
findings.evidence: "Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins." No mechanism specified.
findings.suggestion: Specify the authentication method (e.g., Bearer token/JWT, HMAC-signed requests, mTLS client certificates).

findings.description: Monitoring section describes only Prometheus metric exposure. A production pipeline architecture brief should also describe alerting thresholds, SLA targets, and escalation paths.
findings.evidence: "The pipeline exposes a metrics endpoint at /metrics with standard Prometheus counters for throughput, error rate, and queue depth." No alerting, thresholds, SLAs, or runbook references present.
findings.suggestion: Add alerting rules or runbook reference; add SLA targets for throughput and error rate.

findings.description: Document alternates between precise technical detail and vague placeholders ('Authentication is used', 'the standard retry policy'), signaling incomplete sections.
findings.evidence: Precise: "deduplicates events within a 5-minute window using a Redis cache." Vague: "Authentication is used to verify each request." and "Transformation applies the standard retry policy."
findings.suggestion: Bring vague sections to the same level of technical specificity as detailed sections.

findings.description: Dead-letter queue mentioned for rejected events but no operational handling described (review process, alerting, purge policy).
findings.evidence: "malformed events are rejected with a 400 response and logged to the dead-letter queue." No further DLQ operational description anywhere.
findings.suggestion: Add a DLQ handling policy note.

findings.description: No mention of data retention policy, PII handling, or compliance framework for a pipeline handling customer analytics events.
findings.evidence: No mention of data retention, PII, or compliance anywhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Add a Data Governance or Compliance subsection, or explicitly state governance is out of scope.

findings.description: 'The pipeline and workflow are monitored' — 'and workflow' is redundant.
findings.evidence: "The pipeline and workflow are monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard."
findings.suggestion: "The pipeline is monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard."
